Ayah

بِسْمِ ٱللَّهِ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ ٱلرَّحِيمِ (١)
In the Name of Allah, the All-beneficent, the All-merciful. (1)

Characteristics of Surah al-Hamd

This Surah holds an extraordinary brilliance among the Surahs of the Quran, stemming from the following advantages:

1- The Tone of This Surah

  • This Surah fundamentally differs in tone and melody from other Surahs of the Quran because while other Surahs are all the Word of Allah, this Surah is spoken from the tongue of the servants. In other words, in this Surah, Allah has taught His servants the manner of supplication and speaking with Him.

The beginning of this Surah starts with the praise and glorification of the Lord.

It continues with the expression of faith in the Origin and the End (knowledge of Allah and faith in the Resurrection) and concludes with the requests and needs of the servants.

An aware and awakened person, when reciting this Surah, feels as if they are placed upon the wings of angels, ascending to the heavens, and drawing closer to Allah moment by moment in the realm of spirituality.

It is a very interesting point that Islam, unlike many fabricated or distorted religions that posit intermediaries between "God" and "creation," commands people to establish a connection with their God without any intermediary!

This Surah is a manifestation of this close and direct connection between Allah and man, and the creature with the Creator. Here, one sees only Him, speaks with Him, and hears His message with the ear of the soul; not even any Messenger Prophet or archangel acts as an intermediary in between. And how wondrous it is that this bond and direct connection of the creation with the Creator is the opener of the Holy Quran.

2- Surah al-Hamd is the Foundation of the Quran

  • In a Hadith from the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read: "Al-Hamd is the Mother of the Quran (Umm al-Quran)." This was when Jabir ibn Abdullah al-Ansari came to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said to him:

"Shall I not teach you the most superior Surah that Allah has revealed in His Book?" Jabir said: "Yes, may my father and mother be sacrificed for you, O Messenger of Allah! Teach it to me." So He taught him al-Hamd, the Mother of the Book (Umm al-Kitab). Then He added: "This Surah is a cure for every pain except death." [^1] It is also narrated from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله that He said:

"By the One in Whose hand is my soul, Allah has not revealed in the Torah, nor in the Gospel, nor in the Psalms, nor even in the Quran, anything like this Surah; it is the Mother of the Book." [^2] The reason for this statement becomes clear upon reflecting on the content of this Surah, for it is, in reality, an index of the entire content of the Quran. Part of it is Monotheism (Tawhid) and the knowledge of Allah's attributes; part of it speaks regarding the Resurrection and the Hereafter; and part of it speaks of guidance and misguidance, which is the dividing line between believers and disbelievers. It also contains references to the absolute sovereignty of the Lord, His station of Lordship (Rububiyyah), and His endless blessings, which are divided into two parts: general and specific (the aspects of Rahmaniyyah and Rahimiyyah). Furthermore, it refers to the issue of worship and servitude and its exclusivity to His Pure Essence.

In truth, it expresses the Oneness of Essence (Tawhid al-Dhat), the Oneness of Attributes (Tawhid al-Sifat), the Oneness of Actions (Tawhid al-Af'al), and the Oneness of Worship (Tawhid al-'Ibadah).

In other words, this Surah encompasses the three stages of faith: belief in the heart, confession with the tongue, and action with the limbs. We know that "Umm" means foundation and root.

Perhaps it is for this reason that Ibn Abbas, the famous Islamic commentator, says: "Everything has a foundation... and the foundation of the Quran is al-Fatihah."

Based on these aspects, it is narrated from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله regarding the virtue of this Surah: "Whichever Muslim recites Fatihat al-Kitab, he is given a reward as if he has recited two-thirds of the Quran, and he is given a reward as if he has given charity to every believing man and woman." [^1] The expression "two-thirds of the Quran" is perhaps because one part of the Quran is attention to Allah, another part is attention to the Resurrection, and another part is laws and commands. The first and second parts are present in Surah al-Hamd. The expression "the entire Quran" is because the whole Quran can be summarized from one perspective into faith and action, both of which are gathered in Surah al-Hamd.

3- Surah al-Hamd: The Great Pride of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله

Interestingly, in the verses of the Quran, Surah al-Hamd is introduced as a great gift to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, placed in parallel to the entire Quran, where He says: وَ لَقَدْ آتَيْناكَ سَبْعاً مِنَ الْمَثانِي وَ الْقُرْآنَ الْعَظِيمَ: "And We have certainly given you the seven oft-repeated [verses] and the great Quran" (Surah al-Hijr, Verse 87).

The Quran, with all its greatness, is placed here in parallel to Surah al-Hamd. Its repeated revelation is also due to its extraordinary importance. [^1] This same theme is narrated in a Hadith from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, who said: "Allah the Almighty has specifically favored me with Fatihat al-Kitab and placed it in parallel to the Great Quran, and indeed Fatihat al-Kitab is the most precious of the treasures of the Throne of Allah!" [^2]

4- Emphasis on the Recitation of This Surah

  • Considering the above discussions, which only state a fraction of the virtues of Surah al-Hamd, it becomes clear why there is so much emphasis on its recitation in Islamic Hadiths from both Shia and Sunni sources. Its recitation grants spirit and faith to man, draws him closer to Allah, creates purity of heart and spirituality, strengthens man's will, increases his efforts in the way of Allah and His creation, and creates a distance between him and sin and deviation.

For this reason, we read in a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام: "Iblis shrieked and wailed four times: the first was the day he was cursed; then when he was cast down to the earth; the third was at the time of the appointment (Bi'thah) of Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله after a period of absence of messengers; and the last time was when the Mother of the Book (Umm al-Kitab) was revealed!" [^1]

Content of the Surah

Each of the seven verses in this Surah refers to an important matter:

"بِسْمِ اللَّهِ" is a beginning for every task and teaches us to seek help from the Pure Essence of Allah when starting any work.

"الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ" is a lesson in returning all blessings and the nurturing of all beings to Allah, and paying attention to the reality that all these bounties originate from His Pure Essence.

"الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ" recounts the point that the foundation of His creation, nurturing, and sovereignty is based on mercy and grace, and this principle forms the main axis of the world's educational system.

"مالِكِ يَوْمِ الدِّينِ" is an attention to the Resurrection, the abode of reward for deeds, and Allah's sovereignty over that great court.

"إِيَّاكَ نَعْبُدُ وَ إِيَّاكَ نَسْتَعِينُ" expresses Monotheism in worship and Monotheism in the point of reliance for human beings.

"اهْدِنَا الصِّراطَ الْمُسْتَقِيمَ" expresses the need and love of the servants for the matter of guidance and also an attention to the fact that all guidances are from Him! Finally, the last verse of this Surah is a clear and vivid depiction of the Straight Path; it is the path of those who have been favored with His blessings, and it is separate from the path of those who have earned His anger and those who are astray.

From one perspective, this Surah is divided into two parts: one part speaks of the praise and glorification of Allah, and the other part speaks of the needs of the servant.

As we read in Uyun Akhbar al-Rida عليه السلام in a Hadith from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: Allah the Almighty has said: "I have divided Surah al-Hamd between Myself and My servant; half of it is for Me, and half of it is for My servant, and My servant has the right to ask Me for whatever he wants:

When the servant says 'بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ', Allah the Great says: 'My servant began with My name, and it is upon Me to complete his affairs for him and bless him in all states.' And when he says 'الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ', Allah the Great says: 'My servant praised and glorified Me, and he knew that the blessings he has are from Me, and I also removed calamities from him. Bear witness that I shall add the blessings of the Hereafter to his worldly blessings and ward off the calamities of that world from him just as I warded off the calamities of this world.'

And when he says 'الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ', Allah says: 'My servant bore witness that I am the Compassionate and the Merciful. Bear witness that I shall make his share of My mercy abundant and increase his portion of My gift.'

And when he says 'مالِكِ يَوْمِ الدِّينِ', He says: 'Bear witness that just as he acknowledged My sovereignty and ownership of the Day of Judgment, I shall make his accounting easy on the Day of Reckoning, accept his good deeds, and overlook his sins.'

And when he says 'إِيَّاكَ نَعْبُدُ', Allah the Great says: 'My servant speaks the truth; he worships only Me. I take you as witnesses that for this sincere worship, I shall give him a reward such that all those who opposed this will envy him.'

And when he says 'إِيَّاكَ نَسْتَعِينُ', Allah says: 'My servant has sought help from Me and has taken refuge only in Me. Bear witness that I shall help him in his affairs, come to his aid in hardships, and take his hand on the day of distress.'

And when he says 'اهْدِنَا الصِّراطَ الْمُسْتَقِيمَ...' (to the end of the Surah), Allah says: 'This request of My servant is granted, and he may ask Me for whatever he wants, for I shall respond. I will grant him what he hopes for and secure him from what he fears.'" [^1]

Why is this Surah named Fatihat al-Kitab?

"Fatihat al-Kitab" means the Opener of the Book (the Quran), and it is well-understood from various narrations cited from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله that this Surah was known by this very name even during the time of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله himself.

From here, a window opens toward an important Islamic issue: contrary to what is famous among a group—that the Quran was scattered during the era of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and was later collected during the time of Abu Bakr, Umar, or Uthman—the Quran was collected in this very current form during the time of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله himself, and its beginning was this Surah al-Hamd. Otherwise, neither was this Surah the first Surah revealed to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, nor is there any other reason for choosing the name "Fatihat al-Kitab" for this Surah.

There are numerous other documents at hand that confirm this reality: that the Quran, as the collection we have today, was compiled during the era of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and by His command.

Ali ibn Ibrahim narrates from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام that the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله said to Ali عليه السلام: "The Quran is scattered in pieces of silk, paper, and the like; collect it." Then he adds that Ali عليه السلام rose from that gathering and collected it in a yellow cloth and then sealed it. [^1] Another witness is that Khwarizmi, the famous Sunni scholar, narrates in the book Al-Manaqib from Ali ibn Riyah that Ali ibn Abi Talib and Ubayy ibn Ka'b collected the Quran during the time of the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله.

A third witness is a sentence that Hakim, the famous Sunni author, has brought in the book Al-Mustadrak from Zayd ibn Thabit: Zayd says: "We were in the service of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله collecting the Quran from scattered pieces, and we would place each in its appropriate place according to the guidance of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, but despite this, these writings were separate. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله commanded Ali عليه السلام to collect it in one place and warned us against losing it."

Sayyid Murtada, the great Shia scholar, says: "The Quran was collected during the time of the Messenger of Allah in this very current form." [^1] Tabarani and Ibn Asakir narrate from Sha'bi that six people from the Ansar collected the Quran during the era of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. [^2] And Qatadah narrates that he asked Anas who collected the Quran during the era of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, and he said four people, all from the Ansar: Ubayy ibn Ka'b, Mu'adh, Zayd ibn Thabit, and Abu Zayd. [^3] There are other narrations as well, the citation of all of which would be lengthy.

In any case, in addition to these Hadiths found in Shia and Sunni sources, the choice of the name "Fatihat al-Kitab" for Surah al-Hamd—as we said—is a living witness to prove this subject.

Question: Was the Compilation of the Quran during the Time of the Prophet?

Here, the question arises: how can this statement be believed when it is famous among a group of scholars that the Quran was collected after the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله (by Ali عليه السلام or others)?

In response to this question, it must be said: as for the Quran that Ali عليه السلام collected, it was not just the Quran itself, but a collection of the Quran along with the interpretation (Tafsir), the reasons for revelation (Shan al-Nuzul) of the verses, and the like.

As for Uthman, there are indications showing that Uthman, to prevent differences in recitations, took action to write a single Quran with a specific recitation and diacritical marking (since diacritical marking was not common until then).

As for the insistence of some that the Quran was not collected at all during the time of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and that this honor fell to Uthman or the first and second Caliphs, it might be mostly for the sake of creating virtues; hence, each group attributes this virtue to someone and narrates a report regarding him.

Fundamentally, how can it be believed that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله overlooked such an important task while He paid attention to even very small matters? Is it not that the Quran is the constitution of Islam, the great book of education and training, and the foundation of all Islamic programs and beliefs? Did not its non-collection during the era of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله carry the risk that part of the Quran might be lost or that differences might arise among Muslims?

Furthermore, the famous Hadith of "Thaqalayn," which both Shia and Sunni have narrated—that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said, "I am leaving you and leaving behind two things: the Book of Allah and my progeny"—this itself shows that the Quran had been collected in the form of a book.

And if we see that the narrations indicating the collection of the Quran by a group of companions under the supervision of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله differ in the number of people, it does not create a problem; it is possible that each narration introduces a few of them.

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ (1) الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ (2) الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ (3) مالِكِ يَوْمِ الدِّينِ (4) إِيَّاكَ نَعْبُدُ وَ إِيَّاكَ نَسْتَعِينُ (5) اهْدِنَا الصِّراطَ الْمُسْتَقِيمَ (6) صِراطَ الَّذِينَ أَنْعَمْتَ عَلَيْهِمْ غَيْرِ الْمَغْضُوبِ عَلَيْهِمْ وَ لا الضَّالِّينَ (7)

Commentary

1- بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ

It is a custom among all the people of the world to start every important and valuable task in the name of one of their great figures, and they strike the first shovel of any valuable institution in the name of someone they love; that is, they connect that work with that intended personality from the beginning.

But is it not better that for a program to be enduring and for an organization to remain eternal, we connect it to an Enduring and Eternal Being into Whose Essence non-existence cannot find a way? For all beings in this world move toward wear and decline; only that which is connected to that Imperishable Essence remains.

If the names of the Prophets and Messengers remain, it is due to their connection with Allah, justice, and truth, which do not wear away. And if, for example, the name of "Hatim" is on people's tongues, it is because of his connection with generosity, which is imperishable.

Among all beings, the only one who is Pre-eternal and Everlasting is the Pure Essence of Allah, and for this reason, everything and every task must be started with His name, placed under His shadow, and help must be sought from Him. Therefore, in the first verse of the Quran, we say: "In the name of Allah, the Compassionate, the Merciful" (بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ).

This act should not be only in terms of name and form, but must be connected to Him in terms of reality and meaning, for this connection places it on the correct path and prevents it from any kind of deviation. For this reason, such a task will certainly reach its end and be full of blessing.

For this reason, we read in a famous Hadith from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "Every important matter that is started without the name of Allah is truncated (incomplete)." [^1] The Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, after quoting this Hadith, adds: "Whatever task a person wants to perform, he should say Bismillah, meaning: I start this action with the name of Allah; and every action started with the name of Allah is blessed and auspicious."

We also see Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام saying: "It is appropriate that when we start a task, whether great or small, we say Bismillah so that it may be blessed and auspicious."

In short, the endurance and survival of an action depend on the connection it has with Allah.

On this occasion, Allah the Great, in the first verses revealed to the Prophet, commands that at the beginning of the start of the propagation of Islam, he should start this grave duty with the name of Allah: اقْرَأْ بِاسْمِ رَبِّكَ [^2]. And we see Prophet Noah عليه السلام in that severe and strange storm, when boarding the ark and moving upon the mountain-like waves of water—where he faced many dangers at every moment—commanding his companions to say "بِسْمِ اللَّهِ" at the time of movement and at the time of the ark's stopping, in order to reach the destination and triumph over difficulties (وَ قالَ ارْكَبُوا فِيها بِسْمِ اللَّهِ مَجْراها وَ مُرْساها) (Surah Hud, Verse 41).

And they finally left that perilous journey behind with success and victory and disembarked from the ark with safety and blessing, as the Quran says: قِيلَ يا نُوحُ اهْبِطْ بِسَلامٍ مِنَّا وَ بَرَكاتٍ عَلَيْكَ وَ عَلى‌ أُمَمٍ مِمَّنْ مَعَكَ (Surah Hud, Verse 48).

Solomon also, in the letter he writes to the Queen of Sheba, places its beginning as "بِسْمِ اللَّهِ" (إِنَّهُ مِنْ سُلَيْمانَ وَ إِنَّهُ بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ...) (Surah an-Naml, Verse 30).

And again, based on this principle, all the Surahs of the Quran—start with Bismillah so that the main goal, which is the guidance and leading of humanity toward happiness, is carried out from beginning to end with success and victory and without failure.

Only Surah at-Tawbah is such that we do not see Bismillah at its beginning, because Surah at-Tawbah started with the declaration of war against the criminals of Mecca and the treaty-breakers, and the declaration of war is not compatible with describing Allah as "Compassionate and Merciful."

Here, attention to one point is necessary, and that is: we say Bismillah everywhere; why do we not say Bism al-Khaliq (In the name of the Creator) or Bism al-Raziq (In the name of the Provider) and the like?! The point is that "Allah," as we will soon say, is the most comprehensive of Allah's names and recounts all His attributes at once, whereas other names refer to a specific part of His perfections, such as His creativity, His mercy, and the like.

From what we have said, this reality also became clear: that saying "بِسْمِ اللَّهِ" at the beginning of every task means both "seeking help" by the name of Allah and "starting in His name." These two—namely "seeking help" and "starting"—which our great commentators have sometimes separated from each other, each taking one of the two as implied, return to a single root. In short, these two are inseparable; that is, I both start with His name and seek help from His Pure Essence.

In any case, when we start tasks by relying on the power of Allah—the Allah Whose power is above all powers—it causes us to feel more strength and ability within ourselves psychologically, to be more confident, to strive more, not to fear the greatness of problems, not to become despondent, and meanwhile, to make our intention and action purer and more sincere.

And this is another secret of victory when starting tasks in the name of Allah.

Although no matter how much we speak in the interpretation of this verse, we have said little, for it is famous that "Ali عليه السلام spoke to Ibn Abbas from the beginning of the night until the morning regarding the interpretation of 'بِسْمِ اللَّهِ'; morning came while he had not gone beyond the interpretation of the 'Ba' of 'بِسْمِ اللَّهِ'." But we end this discussion here with a Hadith from that same Imam, and in future discussions, we will have other matters in this regard.

"Abdullah ibn Yahya," who was among the friends of the Commander of the Faithful Ali عليه السلام, came to his service and sat on a throne that was there without saying Bismillah. Suddenly, his body tilted, he fell to the ground, and his head was injured. Ali عليه السلام stroked his head, and his wound healed. Then he said: "Do you not know that the Prophet narrated to me from Allah that every task started without the name of Allah will be without end?" I said: "May my father and mother be sacrificed for you, I know, and after this, I shall not omit it." He said: "In that case, you will be benefited and happy."

Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, when narrating this Hadith, said: "It often happens that some of our Shia omit Bismillah at the start of their work, and Allah faces them with distress so that they may wake up and, meanwhile, this error is washed away from their record of deeds." [^1]

Key Points

1- Is "Bismillah" part of the Surah?!

Among the scholars and learned men of the Shia, there is no disagreement on this issue: that "بِسْمِ اللَّهِ" is part of Surah al-Hamd and all the Surahs of the Quran. Fundamentally, the recording of "بِسْمِ اللَّهِ" in the text of the Holy Quran at the beginning of all Surahs is itself a living witness to this matter, for we know that nothing extra has been written in the text of the Quran, and the mention of "بِسْمِ اللَّهِ" at the beginning of Surahs has been customary from the time of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله until now.

As for the view of Sunni scholars, the author of Tafsir al-Manar has made a comprehensive collection of their sayings as follows:

There is a discussion among scholars whether "بِسْمِ اللَّهِ" at the beginning of every Surah is part of the Surah or not. The earlier scholars of Mecca, including the jurists and Quran reciters such as Ibn Kathir, and the people of Kufa, including reciters like 'Asim and Kisa'i, and some of the companions and followers (Tabi'un) from the people of Medina, as well as Shafi'i in his new book and his followers, and Thawri and Ahmad in one of his two sayings, believe that it is part of the Surah. Also, the Imami scholars and—according to their statement—among the companions, Ali عليه السلام, Ibn Abbas, Abdullah ibn Umar, and Abu Hurairah, and among the Tabi'un scholars, Sa'id ibn Jubayr, 'Ata, Zuhri, and Ibn al-Mubarak have chosen this opinion.

Then he adds that their most important reason is the consensus of all the companions and those who came after them on the mention of "بِسْمِ اللَّهِ" in the Quran at the beginning of every Surah—except Surah al-Bara'ah (at-Tawbah)—while they collectively advised to keep the Quran free from what is not part of it, and for this reason, they did not mention "Amin" at the end of Surah al-Fatihah...

Then he narrates from Malik and the followers of Abu Hanifa and some others that they considered "بِسْمِ اللَّهِ" an independent verse that was revealed to state the beginning of Surahs and the separation between them.

And he narrates from Ahmad (the famous Sunni jurist) and some of the reciters of Kufa that they considered "بِسْمِ اللَّهِ" part of Surah al-Hamd but not part of other Surahs. [^1] From the sum of what has been said, it is understood that even the overwhelming majority of Sunnis consider "بِسْمِ اللَّهِ" part of the Surah.

Here we mention some of the narrations that have been quoted from Shia and Sunni paths in this regard: (and we admit that mentioning all of them is beyond the scope of this discussion and suitable for a full-fledged jurisprudential debate).

1- "Mu'awiyah ibn 'Ammar," one of the friends of Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, says: "I asked the Imam: 'When I stand for prayer, should I recite "بِسْمِ اللَّهِ" at the beginning of al-Hamd?' He said: 'Yes.' I asked again: 'When al-Hamd is finished and I recite a Surah after it, should I recite "Bismillah" with it?' He again said: 'Yes.'" [^2]

2- Darqutni, one of the Sunni scholars, narrates with a sound chain from Ali عليه السلام that a man asked him: "What is the Sab' al-Mathani (Seven Oft-Repeated)?" He said: "It is Surah al-Hamd." The man said: "Surah al-Hamd is six verses." He said: "بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ is also a verse of it." [^3]

3- Bayhaqi, the famous Sunni traditionist, narrates with a sound chain through Ibn Jubayr from Ibn Abbas as follows: "The devilish people stole from the people the greatest verse of the Quran: بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ" (referring to the fact that they do not recite it at the beginning of Surahs). [^1]

Aside from all this, the practice of Muslims has always been to recite "بِسْمِ اللَّهِ" at the beginning of every Surah when reciting the Quran, and it has also been proven through multiple chains (Mutawatir) that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله used to recite it as well. How is it possible for something not to be part of the Quran while the Prophet and Muslims always recite it as part of the Quran and persist in it?

As for the possibility some have raised that Bismillah might be an independent verse that is part of the Quran but not part of the Surahs, it seems to be a very weak and flimsy possibility. For the concept and content of "بِسْمِ اللَّهِ" show that it is for the start and beginning of a task, not that it has a separate and independent concept and meaning. In truth, it is extreme rigidity and prejudice that we would want to propose every possibility just to stand by our word and consider a verse like "بِسْمِ اللَّهِ"—whose content screams that it is a beginning for the discussions after it—as an independent verse cut off from what follows it.

The only notable objection that opponents have in this regard is that they say in the counting of the verses of the Surahs of the Quran (except Surah al-Hamd), "بِسْمِ اللَّهِ" is usually not counted as one verse; rather, the first verse is placed after it.

Fakhr al-Razi has clearly given the answer to this question in Tafsir al-Kabir, where he says: "There is no obstacle to 'بِسْمِ اللَّهِ' being a verse by itself in Surah al-Hamd and being considered part of the first verse in other Surahs of the Quran." (Therefore, for example, in Surah al-Kawthar, "بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ إِنَّا أَعْطَيْناكَ الْكَوْثَرَ" is all considered one verse).

In any case, the matter is so clear that they say: "One day Mu'awiyah, during his reign, did not say 'بِسْمِ اللَّهِ' in the congregational prayer. After the prayer, a group of the Emigrants (Muhajirun) and Helpers (Ansar) shouted: 'Did you steal it or did you forget?'" [^1]

2- "Allah", the Most Comprehensive Name of God

In the phrase "بِسْمِ اللَّهِ", we first encounter the word "Ism" (name), which according to scholars of Arabic literature, is originally derived from "sumuww" (on the weight of ghuluww), meaning height and elevation. The reason every name is called "Ism" is that its concept, after naming, moves from the stage of hiddenness and secrecy to the stage of emergence, appearance, and elevation; or it is because the word finds meaning through naming and comes out of being neglected and meaningless, thus gaining height and elevation. [^2] In any case, after the word "Ism," we encounter the word "Allah," which is the most comprehensive of Allah's names. For a study of Allah's names mentioned in the Holy Quran or other Islamic sources shows that each of them reflects a specific part of Allah's attributes; the only name that refers to all Divine attributes and perfections, or in other words, is comprehensive of the attributes of Majesty (Jalal) and Beauty (Jamal), is "Allah."

For this reason, other names of Allah are often mentioned as attributes for the word "Allah." For example:

"Ghafur" (Forgiving) and "Rahim" (Merciful), which refer to the aspect of Allah's forgiveness (فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ - al-Baqarah, 226).

"Sami'" (All-Hearing) refers to His awareness of things heard, and "Alim" (All-Knowing) refers to His awareness of everything (فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ - al-Baqarah, 227).

"Basir" (All-Seeing) recounts His knowledge of all things visible (وَ اللَّهُ بَصِيرٌ بِما تَعْمَلُونَ - al-Hujurat, 18).

"Razzaq" (Provider) refers to His aspect of providing for all beings, "Dhu al-Quwwah" (Possessor of Power) to His power, and "Matin" (Steadfast) to the firmness of His actions and programs (إِنَّ اللَّهَ هُوَ الرَّزَّاقُ ذُو الْقُوَّةِ الْمَتِينُ - adh-Dhariyat, 58).

And finally, "Khaliq" (Creator) and "Bari'" (Originator) refer to His creation, and "Musawwir" (Fashioner) indicates His shaping (هُوَ اللَّهُ الْخالِقُ الْبارِئُ الْمُصَوِّرُ لَهُ الْأَسْماءُ الْحُسْنى - al-Hashr, 24).

Yes, only "Allah" is the most comprehensive name of God; therefore, we observe that in one verse, many of these names are placed as attributes of "Allah": هُوَ اللَّهُ الَّذِي لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ الْمَلِكُ الْقُدُّوسُ السَّلامُ الْمُؤْمِنُ الْمُهَيْمِنُ الْعَزِيزُ الْجَبَّارُ الْمُتَكَبِّرُ:

"He is Allah, other than Whom there is no deity, the Sovereign, the Pure, the Perfection, the Bestower of Faith, the Overseer, the Exalted in Might, the Compeller, the Superior."

One of the clear evidences of the comprehensiveness of this name is that the expression of faith and Monotheism can only be done with the sentence La ilaha illa Allah; and the sentence "La ilaha illa al-'Alim, illa al-Khaliq, illa al-Razzaq" and the like is not by itself evidence of Monotheism and Islam. It is also for this reason that in other religions, when they want to refer to the Deity of Muslims, they mention "Allah," because describing God as "Allah" is specific to Muslims.

3- The General and Specific Mercy of Allah

What is famous among a group of commentators is that the attribute "Rahman" refers to the general mercy of Allah, which includes friend and foe, believer and disbeliever, and the righteous and the wicked. For we know that "the rain of His boundless mercy has reached everyone, and the table of His ungrudging bounties is spread everywhere." All servants benefit from the various gifts of life and take their sustenance from the spread table of His endless blessings. This is His general mercy that encompasses the expanse of existence, and everyone is immersed in its sea.

But "Rahim" refers to the specific mercy of the Lord, which is exclusive to the obedient, righteous, and submissive servants. For they, by virtue of faith and righteous action, have found the merit to benefit from a specific mercy, forgiveness, and kindness that the polluted and the criminal have no share in.

The only thing that might indicate this point is that "Rahman" has appeared everywhere in the Quran in an absolute form, which is a sign of its generality, while "Rahim" is sometimes mentioned in a restricted form, which is evidence of its specificity, such as: وَ كانَ بِالْمُؤْمِنِينَ رَحِيماً: "And He is ever Merciful to the believers" (al-Ahzab, 43), and sometimes in an absolute form like in Surah al-Hamd.

In a narration from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read that he said: "Allah is the Deity of everything; Rahman to all His creation, and Rahim specifically to the believers." [^1] On the other hand, they have considered "Rahman" to be an intensive form (Sighah al-Mubalaghah), which is another reason for the generality of His mercy, and "Rahim" to be a constant attribute (Sifat al-Mushabbahah), which is a sign of stability and permanence, and this is specific to the believers.

Another witness is that Rahman is among the names exclusive to Allah and is not used for anyone other than Him, while Rahim is an attribute used for both Allah and His servants, as we read about the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله in the Quran: عَزِيزٌ عَلَيْهِ ما عَنِتُّمْ حَرِيصٌ عَلَيْكُمْ بِالْمُؤْمِنِينَ رَؤُفٌ رَحِيمٌ: "Grievous to him is what you suffer; [he is] concerned over you and to the believers is kind and merciful" (at-Tawbah, 128).

Therefore, in another Hadith narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام: "Rahman is a specific name with a general attribute, and Rahim is a general name with a specific attribute." (It is a name specific to Allah, but its concept of mercy encompasses everyone), but "Rahim" is a general name (a name said of both Allah and creation) but refers to the specific mercy of the believers. [^2] Despite all this, we sometimes see that Rahim is also used as a general description; of course, there is no obstacle to the difference mentioned being in the root of these two words, but exceptions also find their way into it.

In the very valuable and famous supplication of Imam Husayn عليه السلام called the Supplication of 'Arafah, we read: "O You Who are the Rahman of this world and the Hereafter, and the Rahim of them both!"

We end our speech in this discussion with a meaningful and expressive Hadith from the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله where He said: "Indeed, Allah the Almighty has one hundred parts of mercy, and He has sent down one of them to the earth and divided it among His creation, by which they show affection and mercy to one another; and He has reserved ninety-nine for Himself, with which He will show mercy to His servants on the Day of Resurrection!" [^1]

4- Why are other Attributes of Allah not mentioned in "بِسْمِ اللَّهِ"?!

This subject is noteworthy: that all the Surahs of the Quran start with Bismillah (except Surah al-Bara'ah, for the reason we said previously), and in "بِسْمِ اللَّهِ", after the specific name "Allah," emphasis is placed only on the attributes of His "Rahmaniyyah and Rahimiyyah." This raises the question: why was no mention made of the rest of the attributes in this sensitive position?

However, by paying attention to one point, the answer to this question becomes clear: that at the beginning of every task, it is necessary to seek help from an attribute whose effects are radiant over the entire world, encompassing all beings and saving those in distress during critical moments.

It is better to hear this reality from the tongue of the Quran where it says: وَ رَحْمَتِي وَسِعَتْ كُلَّ شَيْ‌ءٍ: "And My mercy encompasses all things" (al-A'raf, 156).

And in another place, from the tongue of the carriers of Allah's Throne, we read: رَبَّنا وَسِعْتَ كُلَّ شَيْ‌ءٍ رَحْمَةً: "Our Lord, You have encompassed all things in mercy" (Ghafir, 7).

On the other hand, we see the Prophets seeking refuge in Allah's mercy to save themselves from the clutches of severe and exhausting events and dangerous enemies: the people of Moses, to be saved from the clutches of the Pharaohs, say: "And save us by Your mercy" (Yunus, 86).

Regarding Hud and his followers, we read: فَأَنْجَيْناهُ وَ الَّذِينَ مَعَهُ بِرَحْمَةٍ مِنَّا: "So We saved him and those with him by mercy from Us" (al-A'raf, 72).

Fundamentally, when we seek a need from Allah, it is appropriate to describe Him with attributes that have a connection with that need. For example, Jesus Christ عليه السلام, when requesting the heavenly table (special food), says: اللَّهُمَّ رَبَّنا أَنْزِلْ عَلَيْنا مائِدَةً مِنَ السَّماءِ ... وَ ارْزُقْنا وَ أَنْتَ خَيْرُ الرَّازِقِينَ: "O Allah, our Lord, send down to us a table [spread with food] from the heaven... and provide for us, and You are the best of providers" (al-Ma'idah, 114).

Noah, the great Prophet of Allah, also teaches us this lesson, where he prays for disembarking from the ark in a suitable place: رَبِّ أَنْزِلْنِي مُنْزَلًا مُبارَكاً وَ أَنْتَ خَيْرُ الْمُنْزِلِينَ: "My Lord, let me land at a blessed landing place, and You are the best of those who cause to land" (al-Mu'minun, 29).

And also Zechariah, when requesting a child from Allah to be his successor and heir, describes Allah with the attribute of "the Best of Heirs" and says: رَبِّ لا تَذَرْنِي فَرْداً وَ أَنْتَ خَيْرُ الْوارِثِينَ: "My Lord, do not leave me alone [with no heir], while You are the best of heirs" (al-Anbiya, 89).

Therefore, in the case of starting tasks, when we want to start with the name of Allah, we must seek refuge in His all-encompassing mercy, both His general mercy and His specific mercy. Is there any attribute more suitable than these attributes for progress in tasks and victory over difficulties?!

Interestingly, the force that, like the force of gravity, possesses a universal quality and binds hearts together is this very attribute of mercy. To connect the creation with the Creator, one must also utilize this attribute of mercy.

True believers, by reciting بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ at the beginning of their affairs, detach their hearts from all else and bind them only to Allah, seeking aid and assistance from Him—the Allah whose mercy is all-encompassing, and no creature is deprived of it.

This lesson can also be clearly learned from "Bismillah": that the foundation of Allah's work is based on mercy, while punishment is an exception that will not be realized unless decisive factors for it arise. As we read in the supplication:

يا من سبقت رحمته غضبه:

"O He whose mercy has preceded His wrath." [^1] Human beings must also follow this pattern in their life's program; they should establish the foundation and basis of their actions on mercy and love, reserving the recourse to violence only for times of absolute necessity. The Quran contains 114 Surahs; 113 Surahs begin with "mercy," and only Surah al-Tawbah, which begins with a declaration of war and severity, is without "Bismillah"!

[^1]: Jawshan al-Kabir, Section 20.

Ayah

ٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٢)
All praise belongs to Allah, Lord of all the worlds, (2)

Commentary

The World is Drowned in His Mercy

After "بِسْمِ اللَّهِ", which was the beginning of the Surah, the first duty of servants is to remember the Great Origin of the universe and His endless blessings—those abundant blessings that surround our entire existence and serve as our guide in knowing the Lord, as well as our motivation on the path of servitude.

The reason we say "motivation" is that whenever a human receives a blessing, they immediately want to know the Giver of that blessing, and according to the command of their primordial nature (fitrah), they rise to show appreciation and fulfill the duty of gratitude.

For this reason, scholars of theology (Kalam), in the very first discussion of this science—which speaks of the motivations for knowing God—mention "the necessity of thanking the Benefactor" (وجوب شكر منعم), which is an innate and rational command, as a motivation for knowing God.

And the reason we say His blessings are our guide in knowing the Lord is that the best and most comprehensive way to know the Origin is to study the secrets of creation and the mysteries of existence, especially the presence of blessings in relation to human life.

For these two reasons, Surah al-Fatihah begins with this sentence: (الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ).

To understand the depth and greatness of this sentence, it is necessary to pay attention to the differences between "Hamd" (praise), "Madh" (extolment), and "Shukr" (gratitude) and their results:

1- "Hamd" in the Arabic language means praising someone for a good action or attribute performed by choice. That is, when someone consciously performs a good deed or chooses an attribute for themselves that is the source of voluntary good deeds, we give them "Hamd" and praise.

However, "Madh" means any kind of extolment, whether it is for a voluntary matter or an involuntary one. For example, the description we give of a precious gem is called "Madh" by the Arabs. In other words, the concept of "Madh" is general, while the concept of "Hamd" is specific.

But the concept of "Shukr" is more limited than both of these; we only offer "Shukr" and thanks for blessings that have reached us from another by their will and intention. [^1] If we pay attention to the fact that the "Alif" and "Lam" of "Al-Hamd" is what is called the "Alif and Lam of genus" and here grants the meaning of totality, we conclude that every kind of praise and extolment belongs specifically to Allah, who is the Lord of the Worlds.

Even every human who is a source of good and blessing, every Prophet and divine leader who scatters the light of guidance in hearts, every teacher who teaches, every generous person who bestows, and every physician who places a balm on a painful wound—their praise originates from the praise of Allah. This is because all these favors are originally from His Pure Essence; or in other words, praising them is praising Allah, and extolling them is an extolment for Him.

[^1]: Of course, "Shukr" is more general from one perspective, because gratitude is sometimes with the tongue and sometimes through action, while "Hamd" and "Madh" are usually with the tongue.

Furthermore, if the sun radiates light, the clouds pour rain, and the earth delivers its blessings to us, these too are all from His side. Therefore, all praises return to Him.

In other words, the sentence "الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ" is an allusion to the Monotheism of Essence, Attributes, and Actions (reflect deeply).

2- Fundamentally, describing "Allah" here as "رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ" is actually like mentioning the reason after stating the claim. It is as if someone asks why all praises belong specifically to Allah, and in response, it is said: because He is "رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ" and the Lord of the Worlds.

The Holy Quran says: الَّذِي أَحْسَنَ كُلَّ شَيْ‌ءٍ خَلَقَهُ: "He who perfected everything He created" (As-Sajdah, 7). It also says: وَ ما مِنْ دَابَّةٍ فِي الْأَرْضِ إِلَّا عَلَى اللَّهِ رِزْقُها: "There is no creature on earth but that its provision is upon Allah" (Hud, 6).

3- From the word "Hamd," this point is also well-derived: that Allah has created all these favors and goodness by His own will and choice, contrary to the claims of those who consider God to be a compelled source of grace, like the sun.

4- Interestingly, "Hamd" is not only at the beginning of a task; rather, the end of affairs will also be with the praise of Allah, as the Quran teaches us.

Regarding the people of Paradise, we read: دَعْواهُمْ فِيها سُبْحانَكَ اللَّهُمَّ وَ تَحِيَّتُهُمْ فِيها سَلامٌ وَ آخِرُ دَعْواهُمْ أَنِ الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ: "Their call therein will be, 'Exalted are You, O Allah,' and their greeting therein will be, 'Peace.' And the last of their call will be, 'Praise to Allah, Lord of the Worlds!'" (Yunus, 10).

5- As for the word "Rabb," it originally means the owner and master of something who undertakes its nurturing and reformation. The word "Rabibah," which refers to a man's stepdaughter, is derived from this, because although she is from another husband, she is raised under the supervision of her stepfather.

This word, in an absolute sense, is used only for Allah. If it is applied to other than Allah, it must be in a possessive form (Idafah); for example, we say "Rabb al-Dar" (owner of the house) or "Rabb al-Safinah" (owner of the ship). [^1] In the commentary "Majma' al-Bayan," another meaning is added: a great person whose command is obeyed. However, it is not unlikely that both meanings return to a single root. [^2]

6- The word "'Alamin" is the plural of "'Alam," and "'Alam" means a collection of different beings that possess common attributes or share a common time and place. For example, we say the world of humans, the world of animals, and the world of plants; or we say the Eastern world and the Western world, the world of today and the world of yesterday. Therefore, "'Alam" itself has a collective meaning, and when it is pluralized as "'Alamin," it refers to all the collections of this universe.

Here, the question arises that the plural ending "-in" is usually for rational beings, while not all the worlds of this universe possess intellect. For this reason, some commentators consider the word "'Alamin" here to refer to groups and collections of rational beings,

[^1]: Qamus al-Lughah, Mufradat al-Raghib, Tafsir Majma' al-Bayan, and Tafsir al-Bayan.

[^2]: It should be noted that "Rabb" is from the root "R-B-B," not from the root "R-B-W"; it is what is called "Mudha'af" (doubled), not "Naqis" (defective). However, the concept of nurturing and training is embedded in the original meaning of "Rabb," and therefore in Persian, it is usually translated as "Parvardegar" (Nurturer) (reflect deeply).

such as angels, humans, and jinn.

It is also possible that this plural is due to "Taghlib" (the principle of predominance, where a collection of beings with different attributes is described by the attribute of the superior class).

7- The author of "Tafsir al-Manar" says: It has been narrated from our ancestor Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام—may Allah's pleasure be upon him—that the meaning of "'Alamin" is only humans.

He then adds that in the Quran, "'Alamin" has also appeared with this meaning, such as: "لِيَكُونَ لِلْعالَمِينَ نَذِيراً": "That He may be a warner to the worlds" (Surah al-Furqan, Verse 1). [^1] However, if we consider the instances of the use of "'Alamin" in the Quran, we will see that although the word "'Alamin" in many verses of the Quran means humans, in some cases it has a broader meaning and encompasses humans and other beings of the world, such as: "فَلِلَّهِ الْحَمْدُ رَبِّ السَّماواتِ وَ رَبِّ الْأَرْضِ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ": "Then to Allah belongs [all] praise, Lord of the heavens and Lord of the earth, Lord of the worlds" (Al-Jathiyah, Verse 36).

And like: "قالَ فِرْعَوْنُ وَ ما رَبُّ الْعالَمِينَ قالَ رَبُّ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ وَ ما بَيْنَهُمَا": "Pharaoh said, 'And what is the Lord of the worlds?' [Moses] said, 'The Lord of the heavens and the earth and that between them'" (Verses 23 and 24 of Surah al-Shu'ara).

Interestingly, in a narration that Saduq cited in the book "Uyun Akhbar al-Ridha" from Ali عليه السلام, we read: "The Imam عليه السلام, while interpreting the verse 'الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ', said: 'رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ' refers to the groups of every creature, from inanimate objects to animals." [^1]

[^1]: Tafsir al-Manar, Vol. 1, p. 51.

However, let it not be misunderstood that there is a contradiction among these narrations. For although the concept of the word "'Alamin" is broad and vast, since the "flower of the bunch" of the collection of beings in the world is "man," sometimes the finger is pointed specifically at him, seeing the rest as subordinate and in his shadow. Therefore, if in the narration of Imam al-Sajjad عليه السلام it is interpreted as humans, it is because the primary goal in this great collection is humans.

8- This point is also noteworthy: some have divided the world into two types:

The Macrocosm (al-'Alam al-Kabir) and the Microcosm (al-'Alam al-Saghir). By the Microcosm, they mean the existence of a single human being, because the existence of a human is itself a collection of the various forces that govern this great world, and in truth, man is a sampling of the entire universe.

What causes us to emphasize the broad concept of the world is that it follows the phrase "الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ." In this sentence, we consider all praise and extolment to belong specifically to Allah, then we mention "رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ" as a reason for it, saying:

All praises belong specifically to Him because He is the Owner, Master, and Lord of every perfection, every blessing, and every favor that exists in the world.

Key Points

1- A Red Line over all Patron Deities (Arbab al-Anwa')

The study of the history of religions and sects shows that those who deviated from the line of true monotheism always believed in patron deities (Arbab al-Anwa') for this world. The source of this

[^1]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 1, p. 17.

erroneous thinking was the assumption that each species of being required an independent patron deity to nurture and lead that species. It was as if they did not consider God sufficient for nurturing these species!

They even believed in patron deities for matters such as love, intellect, trade, war, and hunting. For instance, the Greeks worshipped twelve great gods (patron deities) who, in their imagination, held divine banquets atop Mount Olympus, each being a manifestation of one of the human attributes! [^1]

In "Chaldea," the capital of the Assyrian country, they believed in a patron deity of water, a patron deity of the moon, a patron deity of the sun, and a patron deity of Venus, naming each with a specific name, and above all these, they considered "Marduk" to be the Lord of Lords.

In "Rome" as well, multiple gods were prevalent, and the market of polytheism and the multiplicity of gods and patron deities was perhaps hotter than anywhere else.

They divided the total gods into two groups: family gods and state gods, for whom the people did not show much affection (because they were not happy with their government!).

The number of these gods was extraordinarily high, because each of these gods had a specific post and intervened in limited affairs, to the point that the "house door" had its own specific god! Rather, the hinge and the threshold of the house each had a patron deity! According to one historian, it is not surprising that the Romans had 30,000 gods, just as one of their prominent figures jokingly said that the number of gods in our country is such that they are more numerous in the streets and gatherings than the individuals of the nation!

[^1]: A'lam al-Quran, p. 202.

Among these gods, one can name the patron deity of agriculture, the patron deity of the kitchen, the patron deity of the food storehouse, the patron deity of the house, the patron deity of the gas flame, the patron deity of fire, the patron deity of fruits, the patron deity of harvest, the patron deity of the grapevine, the patron deity of the forest, the patron deity of fire (conflagration), the patron deity of the great gate of Rome, and the patron deity of the national fire temple. [^1]

In short, humanity in the past grappled with various superstitions, just as many superstitions from that era remain as a legacy today.

In the era of the Quran's revelation, multiple idols were worshipped and praised, and perhaps all or part of them were successors to the previous patron deities.

Beyond all this, sometimes they practically made humans their "Rabb" (Lord), as the Quran says in rebuking people who considered their "Ahbar" (Jewish scholars) and "Ruhban" (monks and nuns) as their lords: "اتَّخَذُوا أَحْبارَهُمْ وَ رُهْبانَهُمْ أَرْباباً مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ": "They have taken their scholars and monks as lords besides Allah" (At-Tawbah, 31).

In any case, since these superstitions, in addition to leading man to intellectual degradation, are a source of division, discord, and scattering, the divine Prophets rose in intense struggle against them. We see that after "بِسْمِ اللَّهِ," the first verse revealed in the Quran is related to this: "الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ"—all praises belong specifically to Allah, who is the "Rabb" of all the worlds.

In this way, the Quran draws a red line over all patron deities and sends them to the valley of non-existence—their original place—and plants the flowers of monotheism, oneness, solidarity, and unity in their stead.

It is noteworthy that all Muslims are obliged to recite this sentence at least ten times in their daily prayers every day and night and take refuge in the shadow of "Allah," the One God who is the Owner, Lord, Guardian, and Nurturer of all beings, so that they never forget monotheism and do not wander in the bypasses of polytheism.

[^1]: History of "Albert Malet," Vol. 1, Chapter 4, History of Rome.

2- Divine Nurturing as a Path to Knowing God!

Although the word "Rabb," as we said, originally means owner and master, it is not just any owner, but an owner who is responsible for nurturing and training (Tarbiya). For this reason, in Persian, it is translated as "Parvardegar" (Nurturer).

Reflecting on the evolutionary process of living beings, the transformations and changes of inanimate objects, the provision of grounds for the nurturing of beings, and the intricacies hidden in each of these parts is one of the best ways to know God.

The coordinations that exist among the organs of our body, which are often established without our awareness, are one of the living examples of this.

For instance, when an important event occurs in our life and we must rise against it with all our might, in a short moment, a coordinated command is issued to all the organs of our body—unconsciously. Immediately, the heart rate increases, breathing becomes intense, all the body's forces are mobilized, nutrients and oxygen reach all cells abundantly through the blood, nerves become ready for work, and muscles become ready for more movement. Human endurance increases, the sensation of pain decreases, sleep flees from the eyes, fatigue escapes from the limbs, and the feeling of hunger is completely forgotten.

Who creates this amazing coordination in this sensitive moment, with this speed, among all the particles of human existence? Is this nurturing possible from anyone other than the All-Knowing and All-Powerful Allah?! The verses of the Quran are full of examples of this divine nurturing, each of which, God willing, will come in its place, and each is a clear proof for knowing God.

Ayah

ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ ٱلرَّحِيمِ (٣)
the All-beneficent, the All-merciful, (3)

Commentary

We have already discussed the meanings of "Rahman" and "Rahim," the expansion of their concepts, and the differences between these two words in detail during the commentary of "Bismillah," so there is no need for repetition.

A point we must add here is that these two attributes, which are among the most important Divine attributes, are repeated at least 30 times in our daily prayers (we recite them twice in Surah al-Hamd and once in the Surah following it); thus, we praise Allah for His attribute of mercy 60 times.

This is, in fact, a lesson for all human beings to adorn themselves with these Divine ethics in life more than anything else. Furthermore, it is an indication of the reality that if we consider ourselves the servants and slaves of Allah, let it not be that the behavior of merciless owners toward their slaves comes to mind.

In the history of slavery, we read:

"Their owners treated them with extraordinary cruelty and heartlessness, such that it is said if a slave fell short in performing minor services, they would face severe punishments: they were whipped, shackled, tied to mills, assigned to dig mines, imprisoned in terrifying underground dungeons and black holes, and if their sin was greater, they were hanged!" [^1] Elsewhere we read: "Condemned slaves were thrown into cages of wild beasts, and if they survived, another beast would be introduced into the cage."

This was an example of the behavior of owners toward their slaves; however, the Lord of the worlds repeatedly reminds us in the Quran that if My servants act contrary to My command and then become regretful, I will forgive, I will pardon, for I am Merciful and Kind, where He says:

قُلْ يا عِبادِيَ الَّذِينَ أَسْرَفُوا عَلى‌ أَنْفُسِهِمْ لا تَقْنَطُوا مِنْ رَحْمَةِ اللَّهِ إِنَّ اللَّهَ يَغْفِرُ الذُّنُوبَ جَمِيعاً [^2] "Say: 'O My servants who have transgressed against themselves! Despair not of the Mercy of Allah; verily, Allah forgives all sins'" (Repent and benefit from the endless ocean of Allah's mercy).

Therefore, placing "Ar-Rahman" and "Ar-Rahim" after "Rabb al-Alamin" is an indication of the point that while He possesses power over His servants, He treats them with kindness and grace. This nurturing and grace of Allah makes the servants enamored with Him, so that they say "Ar-Rahman Ar-Rahim" with a world of affection.

It is here that man realizes how great a distance there is between the behavior of the Great Allah toward His servants and the method of owners toward their subordinates, especially in the ominous era of slavery.

[^1]: History of Albert Malet, History of the People, Vol. 1, p. 150 (Pocket Edition).

[^2]: Surah Az-Zumar, 53.

Ayah

مَـٰلِكِ يَوْمِ ٱلدِّينِ (٤)
Master of the Day of Retribution. (4)

Commentary

Faith in the Resurrection, the Second Foundation

Here, attention is turned toward the second important principle of Islam, namely the Resurrection and the Day of Judgment, stating: "The Master of the Day of Judgment" (مالِكِ يَوْمِ الدِّينِ‌).

In this way, the axis of the Origin (Mabda') and the Return (Ma'ad), which is the foundation of every kind of moral and social reform, is completed within the human being.

It is noteworthy that the expression "Ownership of Allah" is used here, which specifies His ultimate dominance and influence over everything and everyone on that day—a day when all human beings will be present in that Great Court for reckoning and will stand before their True Master. They will see all their words, deeds, and even their thoughts present; nothing, not even the size of a needle's point, has been destroyed or forgotten, and now it is the human being who must bear the weight of all the responsibilities of his actions!

Even in cases where he was not the direct actor but was the founder of a tradition or a program, he must still accept his share of the responsibility.

Undoubtedly, Allah's ownership on that day is not a conventional ownership, similar to our ownership of what we possess in this world. This is because human ownership is a contractual one; it comes into being with formalities and documents and passes away with other formalities and documents. However, Allah's ownership of the universe is a real ownership; it is that specific bond and connection of beings with Allah, such that if they were severed from Him for a single moment, they would be annihilated—just as if the connection of electric bulbs to the main power plant were severed, the light would vanish and be destroyed at that very moment.

In another sense, this ownership is the result of His Creatorship (Khaliqiyyat) and Lordship (Rububiyyat). He who has created beings and nurtures them under His protection, granting them the grace of existence and being moment by moment, is the True Master of all beings.

We can find a weak example of real ownership within ourselves regarding our own limbs. We are the masters of our eyes, ears, heart, and nerves—not in the sense of conventional ownership, but a type of real ownership that stems from connection, bond, and encompassment.

Here, a question arises: Is Allah not the Master of this entire world, such that we refer to Him as the "Master of the Day of Judgment"?

The answer to this question becomes clear by noting one point: although Allah's ownership encompasses "both worlds," the manifestation and appearance of this ownership are greater on the Day of Resurrection. This is because on that day, all material ties and conventional ownerships are severed, and no one possesses anything of their own there. Even if intercession takes place, it is by Allah's command: يَوْمَ لا تَمْلِكُ نَفْسٌ لِنَفْسٍ شَيْئاً وَ الْأَمْرُ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِلَّهِ‌: "The Day when no soul shall have power to do anything for another, and the command that Day is [entirely] with Allah" (Surah Al-Infitar, Verse 19).

In other words, in this world, a person sometimes rushes to the aid of another, defends him with his tongue, supports him with his wealth, rises to help him with his men and power, and sometimes places him under his protection through various plans and schemes.

But on that day, none of these matters exist. For this reason, when the people are asked: لِمَنِ الْمُلْكُ الْيَوْمَ‌ "To whom belongs the sovereignty this Day?" they say:

لِلَّهِ الْواحِدِ الْقَهَّارِ: "To Allah, the One, the Irresistible!" (Surah Al-Mu'min, Verse 16).

Belief in the Day of Resurrection and faith in that Great Court where everything is precisely calculated has an extraordinarily powerful effect on controlling a human being against wrong and unseemly actions. One of the reasons why prayer (Salat) prevents indecency and evil is that it reminds the human being both of the Origin (Mabda') who is aware of all his deeds and of the Great Court of Allah's Justice.

Emphasis on Allah's ownership of the Day of Judgment also has the effect of challenging the beliefs of the polytheists and those who deny the Resurrection. It is clearly understood from the verses of the Quran that faith in "Allah" was a general belief even for the polytheists of the Age of Ignorance. Thus, when they were asked: "Who is the Creator of the heavens and the earth?" they would say: "Allah!" وَ لَئِنْ سَأَلْتَهُمْ مَنْ خَلَقَ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضَ لَيَقُولُنَّ اللَّهُ‌ (Luqman: 25). However, they would face the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله words regarding the Hereafter with strange disbelief: وَ قالَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا هَلْ نَدُلُّكُمْ عَلى‌ رَجُلٍ يُنَبِّئُكُمْ إِذا مُزِّقْتُمْ كُلَّ مُمَزَّقٍ إِنَّكُمْ لَفِي خَلْقٍ جَدِيدٍ أَفْتَرى‌ عَلَى اللَّهِ كَذِباً أَمْ بِهِ جِنَّةٌ:

"The disbelievers said: 'Shall we point out to you a man who tells you that when you have been torn into pieces [and scattered], you will indeed be in a new creation? Has he forged a lie against Allah, or is there madness in him?'" (Saba, Verse 8).

In a Hadith from Imam Sajjad عليه السلام, we read: When he reached the verse "مالِكِ يَوْمِ الدِّينِ‌", he would repeat it so much that it was as if his soul was about to depart from his body.

كان على بن الحسين اذا قرء مالِكِ يَوْمِ الدِّينِ‌ يكررها حتى يكاد ان يموت‌ [^1]

As for the phrase "يَوْمِ الدِّينِ" (Day of Deen), this expression in all instances where it is used in the Quran refers to the Day of Resurrection, as explicitly mentioned in verses 17, 18, and 19 of Surah Al-Infitar (this expression appears more than ten times in the Holy Quran with this same meaning).

And why is that day introduced as the "Day of Deen"? It is because that day is the Day of Recompense, and "Deen" in the literal sense means "recompense" (Jaza). The clearest program implemented on the Day of Resurrection is this very program of recompense, punishment, and reward. On that day, the veil is removed from deeds, and everyone's actions are precisely calculated, and everyone sees the recompense for their deeds, whether good or bad.

In a Hadith from Imam Sadiq عليه السلام, we read that he said: "يَوْمِ الدِّينِ‌ is the Day of Reckoning." [^2] According to this narration, "Deen" means "reckoning" (Hisab). Perhaps this expression is of the type where the cause is mentioned and the effect is intended, because reckoning is always a prelude to recompense.

Some commentators also believe that the reason for naming the Resurrection "يَوْمِ الدِّينِ" is that on that day, everyone is recompensed according to their religion (Deen) and creed.

However, the first meaning (reckoning and recompense) seems more accurate.

[^1]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 1, p. 19. [^2]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verse being discussed.

Ayah

إِيَّاكَ نَعْبُدُ وَإِيَّاكَ نَسْتَعِينُ (٥)
You [alone] do we worship, and to You [alone] do we turn for help. (5)

Commentary

Man in the Presence of Allah:

This marks the beginning of the servant's needs and his requests from Allah; in fact, the tone of the discourse changes from here. This is because the previous verses were the praise and glorification of the Lord, the declaration of faith in His Pure Essence, and the confession of the Day of Judgment.

However, from here, it is as if the "servant," with this firm foundation of belief and knowledge of the Lord, sees himself in His presence and before His Pure Essence. He addresses Him directly, speaking first of his own servitude to Him and then of His assistance and help: "You alone we worship and You alone we ask for help" (إِيَّاكَ نَعْبُدُ وَ إِيَّاكَ نَسْتَعِينُ‌).

In other words: when the concepts of the previous verses take root in the soul of man, and the depths of his being are illuminated by the light of Allah, the Nourisher of the worlds, and he perceives His general and specific mercy and His ownership of the Day of Recompense, man becomes a complete individual in terms of belief. The first fruit of this deep monotheistic belief is, on one hand, becoming a sincere servant of Allah and emerging from the servitude of idols, tyrants, and lusts; and on the other hand, stretching out the hand of seeking assistance toward His Pure Essence.

In reality, the previous verses spoke of the Monotheism of Essence and Attributes, and here the discourse is about the Monotheism of Worship and the Monotheism of Actions.

Monotheism of Worship means that we consider no one and nothing worthy of worship except the Essence of Allah; we bow only to His command, recognize only His laws, and avoid any kind of servitude or submission to anyone other than His Pure Essence.

Monotheism of Actions means that we consider Him the only true effective agent in the universe (la mu'athira fil-wujud ill-Allah). This does not mean we deny the world of causes or do not seek means; rather, we believe that whatever effect any cause has is by the command of Allah. It is He who gave burning to fire, light to the sun, and life-giving properties to water.

The fruit of this belief is that man will rely only on "Allah"; he considers power and greatness to belong only to Him, and others in his view are ephemeral, perishable, and lacking power.

Only the Essence of Allah is worthy of trust and praise, and He is worthy of man making Him his support in everything.

This thinking and belief detaches man from everyone and all beings and links him only to Allah. Even if he pursues the world of causes, it is by His command; that is, within the layers of causes, he observes the power of Allah, who is the "Causer of Causes."

This belief expands the soul of man and raises the horizon of his thought to such an extent that it connects to infinity and eternity, and sets him free and liberated from limited environments.

Key Points

1- He is the Only Point of Reliance

  • According to what we have studied in Arabic literature, when the "object" (maf'ul) precedes the "subject" (fa'il), it conveys the meaning of exclusivity (hasr). Here, the precedence of the word "Iyyaka" over "na'budu" and "nasta'in" is evidence of exclusivity, and its result is the same Monotheism of Worship and Monotheism of Actions mentioned above.

Even in our servitude and worship, we need His help, and in this path, we must seek assistance from Him, lest we fall into deviation, vanity, hypocrisy, or similar matters that completely destroy our servitude.

In other words, in the first phrase where we say "You alone we worship," there is a slight hint of independence; we immediately correct it with the phrase "إِيَّاكَ نَسْتَعِينُ" (You alone we ask for help), embodying that state of "the matter between two matters" (neither compulsion nor delegation) in our expression, which serves as a model for all our actions.

2- The Use of the Plural Form in "na'budu" and "nasta'in"

This, as well as the subsequent verses which are all in the plural form, shows that the foundation of worship—especially prayer—is based on the collective and the congregation. Even when the servant rises for intimate prayer and supplication before Allah, he must see himself among the collective and the congregation, let alone in other affairs of his life.

Thus, any kind of individualism, going it alone, isolationism, and the like are recognized as rejected concepts from the perspective of the Quran and Islam.

Especially prayer, starting from the Adhan and Iqamah, which are invitations to gather for prayer (Hayya 'ala-s-salah—Hasten to prayer...), to Surah al-Hamd which begins the prayer, and As-salamu 'alaykum which concludes it—all are evidence that this worship essentially has a social aspect; that is, it should be performed in congregation. It is true that individual (furada) prayer is also valid in Islam, but individual worship is considered a secondary and subordinate aspect.

3- Seeking His Help in the Conflict of Forces

In this world, man faces various forces, both in terms of natural forces and his own internal forces. To be able to resist destructive, ruinous, and deviating factors, he needs a helper and supporter. This is where he places himself under the umbrella of the Lord's protection. Every day he rises from sleep and, by repeating the phrase "إِيَّاكَ نَعْبُدُ وَ إِيَّاكَ نَسْتَعِينُ‌," confesses his servitude to the Lord and seeks help from His Pure Essence for victory in this great struggle. In the evening, he lays his head on the pillow repeating the same phrase; he rises with His remembrance and goes to sleep with His remembrance and by seeking help from His existence. Blessed is the one who reaches such a stage of faith; he does not bow his head before any tyrant or powerful person, and he does not lose himself before the attraction of material things. Like the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله of Islam, he says: إِنَّ صَلاتِي وَ نُسُكِي وَ مَحْيايَ وَ مَماتِي لِلَّهِ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ‌: "Indeed, my prayer, my rites, my living, and my dying are all for Allah, the Lord of the worlds" (Al-An'am: 162).

Ayah

ٱهْدِنَا ٱلصِّرَٰطَ ٱلْمُسْتَقِيمَ (٦)
Guide us on the straight path, (6)

Commentary

Treading the Straight Path

After expressing submission before the Lord and reaching the stage of servitude and seeking assistance and aid from His Pure Essence, the first request of the servant is that He guides him to the straight path—the path of purity and goodness, the path of justice and equity, and the path of faith and righteous deeds—so that Allah, who has bestowed all blessings upon him, may also add the blessing of guidance to them.

Although this human being is a believer under such conditions and is acquainted with his God, it is possible at any moment for this blessing to be stripped away from him due to certain factors, causing him to deviate from the straight path and go astray.

Therefore, he must ask his God at least ten times every day and night that no slip or deviation occurs for him.

Furthermore, this "Straight Path," which is the same as the religion of truth, has levels and degrees, and not all individuals are equal in treading these degrees. No matter how many of these degrees a person traverses, there are still higher and loftier degrees that a person of faith must ask Allah to guide him toward.

Here, a well-known question arises: Why do we constantly request guidance to the straight path from Allah? Are we lost?

Moreover, even if this statement is appropriate for us, what does it mean coming from the Prophet and the Imams, who were examples of the perfect human being?!

Answer

Firstly: As mentioned, in the journey of guidance, there is a fear of slipping and deviating at every moment; for this reason, one must place oneself at the disposal of the Lord and request that He keep him firm on the straight path.

We must not forget that our existence, being, and all divine bounties reach us moment by moment from that Great Source.

We previously said that the example of us and all beings (from one perspective) is like electric lamps; if we see the light of the lamp being emitted continuously and uniformly, it is because power reaches it moment by moment from the source of electricity. The power source produces new light every moment, which is delivered to the lamp through the connecting wires.

Our existence is also like the light of these lamps; although it appears as a continuous existence, in reality, a new existence reaches us moment by moment from the Source of Being, the All-Bounteous Creator.

Therefore, just as a new existence reaches us every moment, we are also in need of new guidance. It is obvious that if obstacles are created in the spiritual wires connecting us to Allah, then crookedness, injustices, impurities, and the like will sever our connection with that Source of guidance, and at that very moment, we will deviate from the straight path.

We ask Allah that these obstacles do not arise and that we remain firm on the straight path.

Secondly: Guidance is the very act of treading the path of perfection, where a human being gradually leaves behind stages of deficiency and reaches higher stages.

We also know that the path of perfection is unlimited and continues toward infinity.

Therefore, it is not surprising that even the Prophets and Imams request guidance to the "Straight Path" from Allah, for Absolute Perfection is Allah alone, and everyone without exception is on the path of perfection; what is there to prevent them from also requesting higher degrees from Allah?

Do we not send blessings (Salawat) and greetings upon the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله? Is the meaning of Salawat not a request for new mercy from the Lord for Muhammad and the Family of Muhammad?

Did the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله himself not say: رَبِّ زِدْنِي عِلْماً: "O my Lord! Increase me in knowledge (and guidance)!" Does the Quran not say: وَ يَزِيدُ اللَّهُ الَّذِينَ اهْتَدَوْا هُدىً‌: "And Allah increases in guidance those who are guided" (Maryam: 76).

And it also says: وَ الَّذِينَ اهْتَدَوْا زادَهُمْ هُدىً وَ آتاهُمْ تَقْواهُمْ‌: "As for those who are guided, He increases them in guidance and gives them their piety" (Muhammad: 17).

From this, the answer to the question regarding sending blessings upon the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the Imams also becomes clear: these greetings and blessings are, in fact, a request for a higher and loftier station for those noble personages.

To clarify what we have said, please consider the following two Hadiths:

1- The Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, says in the commentary of the phrase اهْدِنَا الصِّراطَ الْمُسْتَقِيمَ‌:

ادم لنا توفيقك الذى اطعناك به فى ماضى ايامنا، حتى نطيعك كذلك فى مستقبل اعمارنا:

"O Allah, continue for us Your grace (Tawfiq) by which we obeyed You in our past days, so that we may likewise obey You in the future of our lives." [^1]

2- Imam Sadiq عليه السلام says:

ارشدنا للزوم الطريق المؤدى الى محبتك، و المبلغ الى جنتك، و المانع من ان نتبع اهوائنا فنعطب، او ان ناخذ بآرائنا فنهلك‌

"It means: O Allah, keep us firm on the path that leads to Your love, reaches Your Paradise, and prevents us from following our destructive desires or following our own opinions and thus perishing." [^2]

What is the Straight Path?

As it appears from the analysis of the verses of the Holy Quran, the "Straight Path" is the same as the creed of God-worship, the true religion, and adherence to Allah's commands, as we read in Surah

[^1]: Ma'ani al-Akhbar and the Tafsir of Imam Hasan al-Askari, as quoted in Tafsir al-Safi under the aforementioned verse.

[^2]: Ma'ani al-Akhbar and the Tafsir of Imam Hasan al-Askari, as quoted in Tafsir al-Safi.

Al-An'am, verse 161: قُلْ إِنَّنِي هَدانِي رَبِّي إِلى‌ صِراطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ دِيناً قِيَماً مِلَّةَ إِبْراهِيمَ حَنِيفاً وَ ما كانَ مِنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ‌: "Say: Indeed, my Lord has guided me to a straight path: a right religion, the creed of Abraham, the upright, and he was not of the polytheists."

Here, the "firm and established religion, the monotheistic creed of Abraham, and the negation of any kind of polytheism" are introduced as the Straight Path, which specifies the creedal aspect.

However, in Surah Ya-Sin, verses 61 and 62, we read: أَ لَمْ أَعْهَدْ إِلَيْكُمْ يا بَنِي آدَمَ أَنْ لا تَعْبُدُوا الشَّيْطانَ إِنَّهُ لَكُمْ عَدُوٌّ مُبِينٌ وَ أَنِ اعْبُدُونِي هذا صِراطٌ مُسْتَقِيمٌ‌:

"Did I not enjoin upon you, O children of Adam, that you should not worship Satan—for indeed, he is a clear enemy to you—and that you should worship Me? This is the straight path."

Here, the practical aspects of the true religion are pointed out, which is the negation of any satanic act or deviant deed.

And according to the Quran in Surah Al-Imran, verse 101, the way to reach the straight path is through connection and communication with Allah (وَ مَنْ يَعْتَصِمْ بِاللَّهِ فَقَدْ هُدِيَ إِلى‌ صِراطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ‌).

It also seems necessary to mention this point: the straight path is always only one path, because between two points there exists only one straight line, which constitutes the shortest path.

Therefore, if the Quran says the Straight Path is the same as the divine religion and creed in its creedal and practical aspects, it is because that is the closest way of connecting with Allah.

It is also for this reason that the true religion is no more than one: إِنَّ الدِّينَ عِنْدَ اللَّهِ الْإِسْلامُ‌: "Indeed, the religion in the sight of Allah is Islam" (Al-Imran: 19).

By Allah's will, we will see later that Islam has a broad meaning that includes every monotheistic creed in the era and time it was official and had not been abrogated by a new creed.

From this, it becomes clear that the various interpretations narrated by commentators in this regard all actually return to one thing:

Some have interpreted it as Islam.

Some as the Quran.

Some as the Prophet and the truthful Imams.

And some have interpreted it as the religion of "Allah," besides which He accepts no other.

All these meanings return to the same divine religion and creed in its belief-based and practical aspects.

Similarly, the narrations that have been recorded in Islamic sources in this regard, each pointing to an angle of this issue, all return to one principle, including:

It is narrated from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله:

الصِّراطَ الْمُسْتَقِيمَ‌ صراط الانبياء و هم الذين انعم اللَّه عليهم:

"The Straight Path is the path of the Prophets, and they are those who have been encompassed by divine blessings." [^1]

We read from Imam Sadiq عليه السلام that in the commentary of the verse "اهْدِنَا الصِّراطَ الْمُسْتَقِيمَ‌", he said:

الطريق و معرفة الامام:

"It means the path and the knowledge of the Imam." [^2]

And also in another Hadith from the same Imam, it is narrated:

و اللَّه نحن الصراط المستقيم:

"By Allah, we are the Straight Path." [^3]

In another Hadith, again from the same Imam, we read that he said: "The Straight Path is the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام." [^4]

It is certain that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, Ali عليه السلام, and the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام all invited to the same
