monotheistic religion of Allah—an invitation that encompassed both creedal and practical aspects.

Interestingly, Raghib says in the book "Al-Mufradat" regarding the meaning of Sirat: "Sirat is the straight path." Therefore, "being straight" is inherent in the concept of Sirat, and mentioning it as a description (Mustaqim) is for the purpose of further emphasizing this matter.

Ayah

صِرَٰطَ ٱلَّذِينَ أَنْعَمْتَ عَلَيْهِمْ غَيْرِ ٱلْمَغْضُوبِ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا ٱلضَّآلِّينَ (٧)
the path of those whom You have blessed — such as have not incurred Your wrath, nor are astray. (7)

Commentary

Two Deviant Paths!

This verse is, in fact, a clear explanation of the "Straight Path" that we read about in the previous verse. It says: Guide us to the path of those whom You have favored with Your various blessings (the blessing of guidance, the blessing of success, the blessing of the leadership of men of truth, and the blessing of knowledge, action, jihad, and martyrdom), not those who, due to their evil deeds and deviation in belief, incurred Your wrath, nor those who abandoned the road of truth and became lost and wandering in the wilderness (صِراطَ الَّذِينَ أَنْعَمْتَ عَلَيْهِمْ غَيْرِ الْمَغْضُوبِ عَلَيْهِمْ وَ لَا الضَّالِّينَ‌).

In reality, since we do not have complete familiarity with the ways and methods of guidance, Allah commands us in this verse to seek the path and line of the Prophets, the virtuous, and those who have been encompassed by His blessings and favors.

He also warns us that two deviant paths always lie before us: the path of "those who have earned wrath" and the path of "those who are astray," the explanation of which we will soon become familiar with.

Key Points

1- Who are "those You have blessed"?

Surah An-Nisa, verse 69, has explained this group: وَ مَنْ يُطِعِ اللَّهَ وَ الرَّسُولَ فَأُولئِكَ مَعَ الَّذِينَ أَنْعَمُ اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِمْ مِنَ النَّبِيِّينَ وَ الصِّدِّيقِينَ وَ الشُّهَداءِ وَ الصَّالِحِينَ وَ حَسُنَ أُولئِكَ رَفِيقاً.

"And whoever obeys Allah and the Messenger—those will be with the ones upon whom Allah has bestowed favor of the prophets, the steadfast affirmers of truth, the martyrs and the righteous. And excellent are those as companions."

As we observe, this verse introduces the individuals who are the recipients of Allah's blessings as four groups: the Prophets, the Truthful ones (Siddiqin), the Martyrs (Shuhada), and the Righteous (Salihin).

These four stages perhaps point to the meaning that in order to build a healthy, progressive, and faithful human society, first the leaders of truth and the Prophets must enter the field.

Following them are the truthful and honest propagators whose words and deeds are in harmony with one another, so that they may spread the goals of the Prophets everywhere through this means.

Following this period of intellectual construction, naturally, corrupt elements and those who obstruct the path of truth will rise up; a group must stand against them, and some must be martyred so that the tree of monotheism may be irrigated with their pure blood.

In the fourth stage, the product of these efforts and struggles is the emergence of the "Righteous"—a pure and worthy society filled with spirituality.

Therefore, in Surah Al-Hamd, we repeatedly ask Allah day and night to place us in the line of these four groups: the line of the Prophets, the line of the Truthful ones, the line of the Martyrs, and the Righteous. It is clear that at every point in time, we must fulfill our duty and carry out our mission in one of these lines.

2- Who are "those who have earned wrath" and "those who are astray"?

The separation of these two from each other in the above verses shows that each refers to a specific group.

Regarding the difference between these two, three interpretations exist:

1- From the usage of these two words in the Holy Quran, it is inferred that "those who have earned wrath" (Maghdubi alayhim) represents a stage more severe and worse than "those who are astray" (Dallin). In other words, the "Dallin" are the ordinary misguided ones, while the "Maghdubi alayhim" are the stubborn misguided ones or the hypocrites. For this reason, in many instances, the wrath and curse of Allah are mentioned regarding them.

In verse 106 of Surah An-Nahl, we read: وَ لكِنْ مَنْ شَرَحَ بِالْكُفْرِ صَدْراً فَعَلَيْهِمْ غَضَبٌ مِنَ اللَّهِ‌: "But those who open their breasts to disbelief, upon them is wrath from Allah."

And in verse 6 of Surah Al-Fath, it is stated: وَ يُعَذِّبَ الْمُنافِقِينَ وَ الْمُنافِقاتِ وَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ وَ الْمُشْرِكاتِ الظَّانِّينَ بِاللَّهِ ظَنَّ السَّوْءِ عَلَيْهِمْ دائِرَةُ السَّوْءِ وَ غَضِبَ اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِمْ وَ لَعَنَهُمْ وَ أَعَدَّ لَهُمْ جَهَنَّمَ‌: "And [that] He may punish the hypocrite men and hypocrite women, and the polytheist men and polytheist women—those who harbor evil thoughts about Allah. Upon them is a misfortune of evil; and Allah has become angry with them and has cursed them and prepared for them Hell."

In any case, "those who have earned wrath" are those who, in addition to disbelief, tread the path of stubbornness, enmity, and hostility toward the truth, and even do not refrain from harassing and bothering Divine leaders and Prophets whenever possible. Verse 112 of Surah Al-Imran says: وَ باؤُ بِغَضَبٍ مِنَ اللَّهِ‌ ... ذلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ كانُوا يَكْفُرُونَ بِآياتِ اللَّهِ وَ يَقْتُلُونَ الْأَنْبِياءَ بِغَيْرِ حَقٍّ ذلِكَ بِما عَصَوْا وَ كانُوا يَعْتَدُونَ‌: "And they have incurred wrath from Allah... That was because they [the Jews] disbelieved in the signs of Allah and killed the prophets without right. That was because they disobeyed and [habitually] transgressed."

2- A group of commentators believe that "Dallin" refers to the deviants among the Christians and "Maghdubi alayhim" refers to the deviants among the Jews.

This perception is due to the specific stances these two groups took against the call of Islam. As the Quran explicitly recounts in various verses, the deviants among the Jews showed a particular malice and enmity toward the call of Islam. Although initially their scholars were among the heralds of Islam, it was not long before—for reasons not to be detailed here, including the endangering of their material interests—they became the most stubborn enemies and did not refrain from any kind of sabotage in the progress of Islam and the Muslims (just as today the stance of the Zionist group against Islam and Muslims is the same).

In this light, describing them as "those who have earned wrath" seems very accurate. However, it should be noted that this description is actually an application of a general concept to a specific instance, not a restriction of the meaning of "Maghdubi alayhim" to this group of Jews.

As for the deviants among the Christians, whose stance against Islam was not as stubborn to this extent, they were merely caught in misguidance regarding the recognition of the true religion; they are described as "Dallin," which is also an application of a general concept to a specific instance.

In Islamic traditions, "Maghdubi alayhim" has also been repeatedly interpreted as the Jews and "Dallin" as the deviants among the Christians, and the point is exactly what was mentioned above. [^1]

3- There is also the possibility that "Dallin" refers to those who are lost but do not insist on misleading others, while "Maghdubi alayhim" are those who are both lost and misleaders, striving with all their might to make others like themselves!

Evidence for this meaning are the verses that speak of those who obstructed others from the straight path and are mentioned as "يَصُدُّونَ عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ‌" (those who hinder from the path of Allah). In verse 16 of Surah Ash-Shura, we read: وَ الَّذِينَ يُحَاجُّونَ فِي اللَّهِ مِنْ بَعْدِ ما اسْتُجِيبَ لَهُ حُجَّتُهُمْ داحِضَةٌ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ وَ عَلَيْهِمْ غَضَبٌ وَ لَهُمْ عَذابٌ شَدِيدٌ: "And those who argue concerning Allah after He has been responded to—their argument is invalid with their Lord, and upon them is wrath, and for them is a severe punishment."

Nevertheless, it seems that the most comprehensive of these interpretations is the first one—an interpretation in which the other interpretations are gathered and are actually considered instances of it. Therefore, there is no reason for us to limit the broad concept of the verse.

وَ الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ‌.

(End of Surah Al-Hamd)

(2) Surah Al-Baqarah

This Surah was revealed in "Medina" and has (286) verses.

Content of Surah Al-Baqarah

This Surah, which is the longest of the Surahs of the Holy Quran, was certainly not revealed all at once; rather, it was revealed in Medina at various intervals and on occasions of the diverse needs of the Islamic society.

Nevertheless, its comprehensiveness in terms of the fundamental beliefs of Islam and many practical issues (devotional, social, political, and economic) is undeniable.

For in this Surah:

1- There are discussions regarding Monotheism and the recognition of Allah, especially through studying the secrets of creation.

2- Discussions regarding the Resurrection and life after death, especially sensory examples like the story of Ibrahim عليه السلام and the reviving of the birds, and the story of Uzair.

3- Discussions regarding the miracle of the Quran and the importance of this Heavenly Book.

4- Very detailed and lengthy discussions about the Jews and the hypocrites, and their specific stances against Islam and the Quran, and their various types of sabotage in this regard.

5- Discussions regarding the history of the great Prophets, especially Ibrahim عليه السلام and Musa عليه السلام.

6- Discussions regarding various Islamic rulings, including prayer, fasting, jihad in the way of Allah, Hajj, the change of the Qibla, marriage and divorce, rulings on trade and debt, and an important part of the rulings on usury, and especially many discussions regarding spending (Infaq) in the way of Allah, as well as the issue of Qisas (retribution), the prohibition of certain forbidden meats, gambling, wine, and part of the rulings on wills and the like.

As for naming this Surah "Al-Baqarah" (The Cow), it is because of the story regarding the cow of the Israelites, the explanation of which will come in verses 67 to 73, Allah willing.

The Virtue of this Surah

Important narrations have been cited in Islamic sources regarding the virtue of this Surah:

Among them, the late "Tabarsi" in "Majma' al-Bayan" narrates from the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله that he was asked:

"Which of the Surahs of the Quran is the best?" He said: "Al-Baqarah." It was asked: "Which verse of Surah Al-Baqarah is the best?" He said: "Ayat al-Kursi." [^1]

The superiority of this Surah is apparently due to its comprehensiveness, and the superiority of Ayat al-Kursi is due to its specific monotheistic content, which will be discussed in its commentary, Allah willing.

And this does not contradict the fact that some other Surahs of the Quran may have superiority in other aspects, as they are viewed from different perspectives.

Also, it is narrated from Imam "Ali ibn al-Husayn عليه السلام" from the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله that he said: "Whoever recites four verses from the beginning of Surah Al-Baqarah, Ayat al-Kursi and the two verses following it, and three verses from its end, will never see anything unpleasant in his soul or his wealth, Satan will not approach him, and he will not forget the Quran." [^2]

Here, we find it necessary to repeat this important reality: that the significant rewards, virtues, and prizes narrated for the recitation of the Quran or specific Surahs and verses never mean that a person should recite them merely as incantations and be content only with the movement of the tongue.

Rather, reading the Quran is for understanding, and understanding is for reflecting, and reflecting is for action.

Coincidentally, every virtue mentioned for a Surah or a verse has a very high correlation with the content of that Surah or verse.

For example, regarding the virtue of Surah "An-Nur," we read that whoever persists in it, Allah will protect him and his children from being tainted by "adultery" (zina).

This is because the content of Surah An-Nur contains important commands regarding the struggle against sexual deviations: commands to expedite the marriage of single individuals, commands regarding the Hijab, commands to refrain from voyeurism and lustful glances, commands to stop spreading rumors and false accusations, and finally, commands to implement the legal punishment (Hadd) for adulterous men and women.

It is obvious that if the content of this Surah is implemented in a society or a family, there will be no taint of adultery.

Similarly, the verses of Surah Al-Baqarah mentioned above, which happen to be all in the field of Monotheism, belief in the Unseen, the recognition of Allah, and avoiding Satanic whispers—if someone recites them and implements their content in the depths of his soul, he will certainly possess those virtues.

It is true that reciting the Quran has reward in any case, but the main and fundamental reward and the constructive effects will occur when it is a prelude to reflection and action.
